text: The surgeon begins the omentectomy as the first step of the resection, carefully dissecting between the third layer of the omentum and the transverse mesocolon. The video shows a clear demarcation between the yellow transverse mesocolon and the white third layer of the omentum, highlighting the importance of maintaining the correct surgical plane to avoid damaging the mesocolon and its vessels.

The surgeon performs gentle dissection near the head of the pancreas, exposing the confluence of the gastroepiploic vein with the superior mesenteric vein and the loop of Henle. The right gastroepiploic vein is carefully cleared of lymphatic tissue and clipped at its root, followed by the right gastroepiploic artery undergoing the same procedure.

The surgeon dissects the final bit of tissue between the pedicle and the pylorus, ensuring all soft tissue remains with the specimen. The lesser omentum is incised with an adequate margin from the stomach, and the right gastric artery is coagulated at its root, directing all lymphatic tissue towards the specimen.

The surgeon has completed the division of the right gastroepiploic and right gastric pedicles, visibly separating the gastroduodenal junction from surrounding soft tissues. The lesser omentum is dissected superiorly up to the diaphragm's crux. The proximal gastric tumor involves the fundus and body, with soft tissue being removed from the diaphragm's crux and kept with the specimen. The left gastric vein is dissected, and the stomach is divided at its junction with the first part of the duodenum.

The surgeon bares the left gastric artery of all lymphatic tissue, pushing it towards the specimen, and applies a clip at the artery's origin. After ligating the left gastric artery and vein, the surgeon encounters flimsy adhesions between the stomach and the pancreas. Using a ligature, the surgeon carefully separates these adhesions, ensuring no tumor tissue remains on the pancreas while avoiding pancreatic injury.

The surgeon divides the remaining adhesions with the diaphragm's crura, ensuring all tissue affected by chemotherapy is removed with the specimen. The dissection involves dividing the short gastric vessels from the medial side along the greater curvature of the stomach, requiring careful navigation to avoid injury to the spleen and prevent incomplete vessel division that could cause bleeding.

A gauze piece is placed at the hilum of the spleen to maintain dryness. The stomach is fully mobilized, with surrounding soft tissue and vessels taken at their roots. The surgeon proceeds to remove lymph nodes along the common hepatic artery, extending to the right side of the right hepatic artery.
